# HLD Section Template

## Contents
- Purpose
- Contract gates
- Section template
- Acceptance criteria patterns
- Sizing and readiness checks

## Purpose
Use this format for each HLD section within `plan/hld.md`.

HLD sections define the high-level design for one implementation unit within a feature area. They describe the desired outcome and architectural approach at enough detail for the architect to conduct targeted codebase research and produce an LLD.

**LLDs are NOT created here.** LLDs are generated by the sdlc-architect during execution, after deep research into the actual codebase. The planner's role ends at HLD.

## Contract gates
- REQUIRE parent user story linkage and PRD trace.
- REQUIRE explicit acceptance criteria before declaring the section complete.
- REQUIRE overlap check against sibling HLD sections.
- DENY acceptance criteria that are not observable or testable.

## Section template

### 1) Outcome statement
- Describe the user/system outcome this HLD section delivers in one sentence.
- Focus on what is observable when done, not how to implement it.

### 2) Parent linkage
- Parent User Story reference (US-NNN).
- Trace to PRD section/requirement.

### 3) Scope
- What this HLD section includes.
- What this HLD section explicitly does not include (to prevent scope creep).

### 4) High-level design
- Architecture approach for this implementation unit.
- Key components and their responsibilities.
- Key interfaces and data contracts between components.
- Technology choices from the PRD/architecture that apply to this unit.
- Integration points with external systems or other HLD sections.

### 5) Acceptance criteria
- Use testable, observable conditions.
- Prefer Given/When/Then format or explicit bullet checks.
- Include error cases and boundary conditions.
- Include non-functional requirements (performance threshold, security behavior) when relevant.

### 6) Dependencies and blockers
- Upstream HLD sections that must be completed first.
- External dependencies (APIs, libraries, infrastructure).
- Known blockers and their owners.

### 7) Traceability and overlap control
- Parent User Story ID and PRD requirement trace.
- Sibling HLD section non-overlap statement.

## Acceptance criteria patterns
- REQUIRE functional behavior is observable in the target environment.
- REQUIRE error cases and boundary conditions are included.
- REQUIRE non-functional expectations (performance, security, offline behavior) when relevant to this unit.
- REQUIRE evidence format for closure (test pass, metric threshold, review artifact).

## Sizing and readiness checks
- REQUIRE the HLD section is scoped small enough for one focused architect-led design and implementation cycle.
- REQUIRE the outcome statement is unambiguous.
- REQUIRE dependencies are known or explicitly tracked as blockers.
- REQUIRE acceptance criteria are testable before this section is considered complete.
- REQUIRE trace link back to parent User Story and PRD requirement.
